Evaluation of the Mean Cycle Time
in Stochastic Discrete Event Dynamic Systems‚Ä†‚Ä†thanks: Proc. 6th Intern. Conf. on Queueing Theory and Network Applications (QTNA‚Äô11), ACM, New York, 2011, pp.¬†93‚Äď100.

Nikolai Krivulin Faculty of Mathematics and Mechanics, St.¬†Petersburg State University, 28 Universitetsky Ave., St.¬†Petersburg, 198504, Russia, nkk@math.spbu.ru.The work was partially supported by the Russian Foundation for Basic Research under Grant #09-01-00808.
Abstract

We consider stochastic discrete event dynamic systems that have time evolution represented with two-dimensional state vectors through a vector equation that is linear in terms of an idempotent semiring. The state transitions are governed by second-order random matrices that are assumed to be independent and identically distributed. The problem of interest is to evaluate the mean growth rate of state vector, which is also referred to as the mean cycle time of the system, under various assumptions on the matrix entries.

We give an overview of early results including a solution for systems determined by matrices with independent entries having a common exponential distribution. It is shown how to extend the result to the cases when the entries have different exponential distributions and when some of the entries are replaced by zero. Finally, the mean cycle time is calculated for systems with matrices that have one random entry, whereas the other entries in the matrices can be arbitrary nonnegative and zero constants. The random entry is always assumed to have exponential distribution except for one case of a matrix with zero row when the particular form of the matrix makes it possible to obtain a solution that does not rely on exponential distribution assumptions.

Key-Words: discrete event dynamic system, mean cycle time, idempotent semiring, random matrix, convergence in distribution.

1 Introduction

Many actual systems in engineering, manufacturing, management, and other areas can be modeled as stochastic discrete event dynamic systems. The dynamics of the systems is normally described by recurrent equations for a set of state variables representing the occurrence time of system events. In some cases, the state evolution of the system can also be written in vector form with a state transition matrix through a vector equation that is linear in the sense of an idempotent semiring [1, 2, 3, 4]. As an example, one can consider the G/G/1 queue and related representation in the form of Lindley recursions (see, e.g. [5]).

In the analysis of system performance, one is often interested in evaluating the mean growth rate of the system state vector, which presents the mean time of one system operation cycle. However, exact calculation of the mean cycle time can be a rather cumbersome problem even for quite simple systems. Most of the known results are limited to systems with second-order state transition matrices having independent random entries.

In this paper we give an overview of existing solutions and present new results for the problem. We start with motivating examples of calculating the mean cycle time drawn from manufacturing and telecommunications. Early results from [6, 7, 8] including solutions for second-order matrices with independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) entries are outlined. For exponential, continuous uniform, Bernoulli, geometric, or discrete uniform distributions, the solutions are given as rational functions of distribution parameters.

To get solutions for other systems with second-order matrices, we implement an approach based on constructing and analyzing a sequence of one-dimensional distributions. The mean cycle time is calculated as the mean of a random variable (r.v.) determined by the limiting distribution of the sequence. Specifically, the case of a matrix where the entries have exponential distributions with arbitrary parameters is considered. We describe a computational technique [9] that actually reduces the problem to the solution of an appropriate set of linear algebraic equations.

Furthermore, systems with random matrices that can have some constant entries are examined. Both recent results for matrices with zero entries [10, 11] and new results for matrices that can have arbitrary nonnegative constant entries are presented. The new results include solutions for systems with second-order matrices that have one random entry, whereas the other entries can be arbitrary nonnegative and zero constants. The random entry is always assumed to have exponential distribution except for one case when the particular form of the matrix makes it possible to obtain a solution that does not rely on exponential distribution assumptions.

2 Preliminary Observations

2.1 Motivating Examples

We start with example systems drawn from manufacturing and telecommunications. Other related examples can be found in [6, 8, 3].

2.1.1 Manufacturing

Consider a manufacturing system that consists of two production centers AūĚźīA and BūĚźĶB. Each center produces its output based on use of an output from the other center. The operation of each center as well as of the entire system consists in a sequence of production cycles. Every cycle involves simultaneous production of a new output and transportation of the output by the previous cycle. A center completes its current cycle as soon as it finishes production of the current output and an output from the other center arrives. A current production cycle of the system comes to the end and the next cycle is initiated as soon as both centers AūĚźīA and BūĚźĶB complete their respective production cycles.

Suppose that at the initial time, each center has its output available for delivery. Given random production and transportation time for both centers, one is often interested in evaluating the mean time of production cycle as the number of cycles goes to infinity. The system throughput defined as the inverse of the mean cycle time can be another performance measure of interest.

For every cycle k=1,2,‚Ä¶ūĚĎė12‚Ä¶k=1,2,\ldots we introduce the following notation. Let őĪksubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė\alpha_{k} and őīksubscriptūĚõŅūĚĎė\delta_{k} be r.v.‚Äôs that represent the production time at respective centers AūĚźīA and BūĚźĶB, and let ő≤ksubscriptūĚõĹūĚĎė\beta_{k} and ő≥ksubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎė\gamma_{k} be r.v.‚Äôs that represent the transportation time from BūĚźĶB to AūĚźīA and from AūĚźīA to BūĚźĶB. By x‚Äč(k)ūĚĎ•ūĚĎėx(k) and y‚Äč(k)ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎėy(k) we denote the cycle completion epochs at centers AūĚźīA and BūĚźĶB.

With the condition that x‚Äč(0)=y‚Äč(0)=0ūĚĎ•0ūĚĎ¶00x(0)=y(0)=0, the dynamics of the system is naturally represented by two equations

x‚Äč(k)=max‚Ā°(x‚Äč(k‚ąí1)+őĪk,y‚Äč(k‚ąí1)+ő≤k),y‚Äč(k)=max‚Ā°(x‚Äč(k‚ąí1)+ő≥k,y‚Äč(k‚ąí1)+őīk).formulae-sequenceūĚĎ•ūĚĎėūĚĎ•ūĚĎė1subscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎ¶ūĚĎė1subscriptūĚõĹūĚĎėūĚĎ¶ūĚĎėūĚĎ•ūĚĎė1subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎėūĚĎ¶ūĚĎė1subscriptūĚõŅūĚĎė\displaystyle\begin{split}x(k)&=\max(x(k-1)+\alpha_{k},y(k-1)+\beta_{k}),\\ y(k)&=\max(x(k-1)+\gamma_{k},y(k-1)+\delta_{k}).\end{split} (1)

The mean cycle time for the system is given by

őĽ=limk‚Üí‚ąě1k‚Äčmax‚Ā°(x‚Äč(k),y‚Äč(k)),ūĚúÜsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎė1ūĚĎėūĚĎ•ūĚĎėūĚĎ¶ūĚĎė\lambda=\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{1}{k}\max(x(k),y(k)), (2)

provided that the limit on the right exists.

2.1.2 Telecommunications

Suppose that there is a system of two work stations AūĚźīA and BūĚźĶB which exchange messages over a communication network. Each station generates and sends messages in response to messages received from the other station. If a message is sent from a station, it immediately enters the network and then goes from one intermediate node to another until it arrives at the other station.

The operation of each station and of the entire system consists in a sequence of communication sessions. A new session at a station begins with generation of a new message and completes by sending the message to the other station. Once the message is generated, it is sent from the station as soon as a message from the other station arrives. A current communication session of the system starts when both nodes AūĚźīA and BūĚźĶB complete their previous sessions, and lasts until they complete the current sessions.

For every session k=1,2,‚Ä¶ūĚĎė12‚Ä¶k=1,2,\ldots we denote by x‚Äč(k)ūĚĎ•ūĚĎėx(k) and y‚Äč(k)ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎėy(k) the session completion epochs for respective stations AūĚźīA and BūĚźĶB. Let őĪksubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė\alpha_{k} and őīksubscriptūĚõŅūĚĎė\delta_{k} be the random message generation time for stations AūĚźīA and BūĚźĶB, and ő≤ksubscriptūĚõĹūĚĎė\beta_{k} and ő≥ksubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎė\gamma_{k} be the random message transmission time from BūĚźĶB to AūĚźīA and from AūĚźīA to BūĚźĶB.

Given the r.v.‚Äôs őĪksubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė\alpha_{k}, ő≤ksubscriptūĚõĹūĚĎė\beta_{k}, ő≥ksubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎė\gamma_{k}, and őīksubscriptūĚõŅūĚĎė\delta_{k} for all k=1,2,‚Ä¶ūĚĎė12‚Ä¶k=1,2,\ldots we consider the problem of evaluating the mean communication session time which one can take as a natural performance measure for the system. As it is easy to see, the dynamic equations for the system have the same form as (1). Moreover, the mean communication session time can be given by (2) as well.

2.2 Problem Representation and Preliminary Analysis

Consider an idempotent semiring with two operations, maximum as semiring addition, and arithmetic addition as semiring multiplication. Define a system state transition matrix for every k=1,2,‚Ä¶ūĚĎė12‚Ä¶k=1,2,\ldots as

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪkő≤kő≥kőīk).ūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõĹūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõŅūĚĎėA(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&\beta_{k}\\ \gamma_{k}&\delta_{k}\end{array}\right). (3)

We suppose that each of the sequences {őĪk}subscriptūĚõľūĚĎė\{\alpha_{k}\}, {ő≤k}subscriptūĚõĹūĚĎė\{\beta_{k}\}, {ő≥k}subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎė\{\gamma_{k}\}, and {őīk}subscriptūĚõŅūĚĎė\{\delta_{k}\} involves either i.i.d. r.v.‚Äôs or nonnegative constants, and that the sequences are independent themselves.

Furthermore, we introduce a state vector ūĚíõ‚Äč(k)ūĚíõūĚĎė\bm{z}(k) as follows

ūĚíõ‚Äč(k)=(x‚Äč(k)y‚Äč(k)),ūĚíõ‚Äč(0)=(00).formulae-sequenceūĚíõūĚĎėūĚĎ•ūĚĎėūĚĎ¶ūĚĎėūĚíõ000\bm{z}(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{c}x(k)\\ y(k)\end{array}\right),\qquad\bm{z}(0)=\left(\begin{array}[]{c}0\\ 0\end{array}\right).

Scalar equations (1) can now be replaced with an equation

ūĚíõ‚Äč(k)=A‚Äč(k)‚ÄčūĚíõ‚Äč(k‚ąí1),ūĚíõūĚĎėūĚźīūĚĎėūĚíõūĚĎė1\bm{z}(k)=A(k)\bm{z}(k-1), (4)

where matrix-vector multiplication is thought of in the sense of addition and multiplication in the semiring.

With the norm ‚ą•‚čÖ‚ą•\|\cdot\| defined as the maximum entry of a vector, we can rewrite the limit in (2) to represent the problem of interest as that of finding

őĽ=limk‚Üí‚ąě‚ÄĖūĚíõ‚Äč(k)‚ÄĖ1/k,ūĚúÜsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎėsuperscriptnormūĚíõūĚĎė1ūĚĎė\lambda=\lim_{k\to\infty}\|\bm{z}(k)\|^{1/k}, (5)

where őĽūĚúÜ\lambda can generally be interpreted as the mean growth rate of state vector in system (4).

Consider a stochastic system governed by equation (4) and assume that the random entries in matrix (3) have finite means. It is not difficult to verify (see e.g. [12, 5]) that under this assumption, the limit at (5) exists with probability one. Moreover, in this case, it holds that

őĽ=limk‚Üí‚ąěūĚĖ§‚Äč‚ÄĖūĚíõ‚Äč(k)‚ÄĖ1/k,ūĚúÜsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎėūĚĖ§superscriptnormūĚíõūĚĎė1ūĚĎė\lambda=\lim_{k\to\infty}\mathsf{E}\|\bm{z}(k)\|^{1/k},

or, in ordinary notation, that

őĽ=limk‚Üí‚ąě1k‚ÄčūĚĖ§‚Äčmax‚Ā°(x‚Äč(k),y‚Äč(k)).ūĚúÜsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎė1ūĚĎėūĚĖ§ūĚĎ•ūĚĎėūĚĎ¶ūĚĎė\lambda=\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{1}{k}\mathsf{E}\max(x(k),y(k)).

Note that the above vector representation, among other benefits, enables one to enhance the analysis of the system by using models and methods of idempotent algebra (see e.g., [5]). Specifically, the range of particular cases to analyze can be reduced on the basis of the following observations. First, it is not difficult to see from (4) and (5) that both systems, with a matrix A‚Äč(k)ūĚźīūĚĎėA(k) and its transpose AT‚Äč(k)superscriptūĚźīūĚĎáūĚĎėA^{T}(k), have the same value of őĽūĚúÜ\lambda. Moreover, the mean cycle time is invariant to simultaneous row and column permutations in A‚Äč(k)ūĚźīūĚĎėA(k). Finally, any superposition of the above transformations also leaves őĽūĚúÜ\lambda unchanged.

2.3 Previous Results

The problem of evaluating the mean cycle time may involve cumbersome computations even for quite simple systems. Many known results are obtained for systems with second-order matrices with i.i.d. random entries [7, 6, 8].

A system with a matrix where all entries are exponentially distributed with parameter őľūĚúá\mu is examined in [7, 6]. A somewhat too complicated solution based on the theory of Markov chains in [6] leads to the result őĽ=407/(228‚Äčőľ)ūĚúÜ407228ūĚúá\lambda=407/(228\mu).

For the particular case őľ=1ūĚúá1\mu=1, this result is obtained in [7] with a less rigorous approach that actually reduces the problem to the solution of an integral equation.

Furthermore, the technique developed in [6] is extended to get the solution őĽ=0.719ūĚúÜ0.719\lambda=0.719 when all entries of the matrix have a continuous uniform distribution on [0,1]01[0,1].

Another rather sophisticated approach to the problem with second-order matrices is proposed in [8]. The approach considers a sequence of two-dimensional distributions of the system state vector and examines the convergence of the sequence by applying the Laplace transform of the distributions. It is shown that for a system with a matrix where the diagonal and off-diagonal entries are exponentially distributed with respective parameters őľūĚúá\mu and őĹūĚúą\nu, the solution takes the form

őĽ=P‚Äč(őľ,őĹ)/Q‚Äč(őľ,őĹ),ūĚúÜūĚĎÉūĚúáūĚúąūĚĎĄūĚúáūĚúą\lambda=P(\mu,\nu)/Q(\mu,\nu),

where

P‚Äč(őľ,őĹ)ūĚĎÉūĚúáūĚúą\displaystyle P(\mu,\nu) =160‚Äčőľ10+1776‚Äčőľ9‚ÄčőĹ+8220‚Äčőľ8‚ÄčőĹ2+21378‚Äčőľ7‚ÄčőĹ3absent160superscriptūĚúá101776superscriptūĚúá9ūĚúą8220superscriptūĚúá8superscriptūĚúą221378superscriptūĚúá7superscriptūĚúą3\displaystyle=160\mu^{10}+1776\mu^{9}\nu+8220\mu^{8}\nu^{2}+21378\mu^{7}\nu^{3}
+35595‚Äčőľ6‚ÄčőĹ4+41566‚Äčőľ5‚ÄčőĹ5+35595‚Äčőľ4‚ÄčőĹ635595superscriptūĚúá6superscriptūĚúą441566superscriptūĚúá5superscriptūĚúą535595superscriptūĚúá4superscriptūĚúą6\displaystyle\phantom{=}+35595\mu^{6}\nu^{4}+41566\mu^{5}\nu^{5}+35595\mu^{4}\nu^{6}
+21378‚Äčőľ3‚ÄčőĹ7+8220‚Äčőľ2‚ÄčőĹ8+1776‚Äčőľ‚ÄčőĹ9+160‚ÄčőĹ10,21378superscriptūĚúá3superscriptūĚúą78220superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúą81776ūĚúásuperscriptūĚúą9160superscriptūĚúą10\displaystyle\phantom{=}+21378\mu^{3}\nu^{7}+8220\mu^{2}\nu^{8}+1776\mu\nu^{9}+160\nu^{10},
Q‚Äč(őľ,őĹ)ūĚĎĄūĚúáūĚúą\displaystyle Q(\mu,\nu) =16őľőĹ(őľ+őĹ)(8őľ8+80őľ7őĹ+321őľ6őĹ2+690őľ5őĹ3\displaystyle=16\mu\nu(\mu+\nu)(8\mu^{8}+80\mu^{7}\nu+321\mu^{6}\nu^{2}+690\mu^{5}\nu^{3}
+880őľ4őĹ4+690őľ3őĹ5+321őľ2őĹ6+80őľőĹ7+8őĹ8).\displaystyle\phantom{=}+880\mu^{4}\nu^{4}+690\mu^{3}\nu^{5}+321\mu^{2}\nu^{6}+80\mu\nu^{7}+8\nu^{8}).

For a system with a symmetric matrix having both diagonal entries exponentially distributed with parameter őľūĚúá\mu and off-diagonal entries equal to zero, the solution is őĽ=5/(4‚Äčőľ)ūĚúÜ54ūĚúá\lambda=5/(4\mu).

We conclude with an overview of results for discrete distributions. In [7, 6], the case of matrices of order n=2,3ūĚĎõ23n=2,3 is examined when the entries have the discrete uniform distribution on 0,1,‚Ä¶,m01‚Ä¶ūĚĎö0,1,\ldots,m. The evolution of the differences between components of the state vector is represented as a Markov chain, whereas the mean cycle time is evaluated from the stationary probabilities of the chain. The results obtained can be summarized as follows

n=2,m=1,formulae-sequenceūĚĎõ2ūĚĎö1\displaystyle n=2,\quad m=1, őĽūĚúÜ\displaystyle\lambda =6/7‚Čą0.857,absent670.857\displaystyle=6/7\approx 0.857,
n=2,m=2,formulae-sequenceūĚĎõ2ūĚĎö2\displaystyle n=2,\quad m=2, őĽūĚúÜ\displaystyle\lambda =0.803,absent0.803\displaystyle=0.803,
n=2,m‚Üí‚ąě,formulae-sequenceūĚĎõ2‚ÜíūĚĎö\displaystyle n=2,\quad m\to\infty, őĽūĚúÜ\displaystyle\lambda ‚Üí0.719,‚Üíabsent0.719\displaystyle\to 0.719,
n=3,m=1,formulae-sequenceūĚĎõ3ūĚĎö1\displaystyle n=3,\quad m=1, őĽūĚúÜ\displaystyle\lambda =0.979.absent0.979\displaystyle=0.979.

The above approach is further developed in [8] where a technique is implemented based on recursive computation of joint generating function of the state vector. In the case of a matrix with i.i.d. entries having Bernoulli distribution with parameter pūĚĎĚp, the solution is given by

őĽ=1‚ąí(1+2‚Äčp)‚Äč(1‚ąíp)41‚ąí2‚Äčp‚Äč(1‚ąíp)‚Äč(1‚ąí3‚Äčp+p2).ūĚúÜ112ūĚĎĚsuperscript1ūĚĎĚ412ūĚĎĚ1ūĚĎĚ13ūĚĎĚsuperscriptūĚĎĚ2\lambda=1-\frac{(1+2p)(1-p)^{4}}{1-2p(1-p)(1-3p+p^{2})}.

Finally, for the system where the entries of the matrix are geometrically distributed with parameter pūĚĎĚp, it holds that

őĽ=N‚Äč(p)/D‚Äč(p),ūĚúÜūĚĎĀūĚĎĚūĚź∑ūĚĎĚ\lambda=N(p)/D(p),

where

N‚Äč(p)ūĚĎĀūĚĎĚ\displaystyle N(p) =p(4+18p+50p2+99p3+175p4+244p5+289p6\displaystyle=p(4+18p+50p^{2}+99p^{3}+175p^{4}+244p^{5}+289p^{6}
+273p7+218p8+137p9+77p10+32p11+11p12+p13),\displaystyle\phantom{=}+273p^{7}+218p^{8}+137p^{9}+77p^{10}+32p^{11}+11p^{12}+p^{13}),
D‚Äč(p)ūĚź∑ūĚĎĚ\displaystyle D(p) =(1‚ąíp)(1+p)(1+p+p2)(1+6p+8p2+20p3\displaystyle=(1-p)(1+p)(1+p+p^{2})(1+6p+8p^{2}+20p^{3}
+25p4+32p5+25p6+20p7+8p8+6p9+p10).\displaystyle\phantom{=}+25p^{4}+32p^{5}+25p^{6}+20p^{7}+8p^{8}+6p^{9}+p^{10}).

3 An Overview of Recent Results

This section offers solutions that generalize results given in [7, 6, 8] for systems that have second-order matrices with exponentially distributed entries. We start with a solution developed in [9] for the case of pure random matrices having only random entries with different exponential distributions.

Furthermore, we consider results obtained in [10, 11] for matrices with some nonrandom entries that are equal to zero. The reduced number of random entries in the matrices allows one to simplify the evaluation of the mean cycle time and usually gives quite compact results.

3.1 The System With a Pure Random Matrix

Consider a system with the state transition matrix

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪkő≤kő≥kőīk),ūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõĹūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõŅūĚĎėA(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&\beta_{k}\\ \gamma_{k}&\delta_{k}\end{array}\right),

where őĪksubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė\alpha_{k}, ő≤ksubscriptūĚõĹūĚĎė\beta_{k}, ő≥ksubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎė\gamma_{k}, and őīksubscriptūĚõŅūĚĎė\delta_{k} are assumed to have exponential distributions with respective parameters őľūĚúá\mu, őĹūĚúą\nu, ŌÉūĚúé\sigma, and ŌĄūĚúŹ\tau.

To get the mean cycle time, a computational technique proposed in [9] reduces the problem to the solution of a system of linear equations, accompanied by the evaluation of a linear functional of the solution. The technique leans upon construction and examination of a sequence of probability density functions. It is shown that there is a one-to-one correspondence between the sequence of density functions and a sequence of certain vectors in a vector space. The correspondence is then exploited to provide for the result in terms of algebraic computations.

Based on the above technique, the mean cycle time is evaluated as follows. First we introduce the vectors

ūĚĚé1=(ŌČ10,ŌČ11,ŌČ12,ŌČ13)T,ūĚĚé2=(ŌČ20,ŌČ21,ŌČ22,ŌČ23)T.formulae-sequencesubscriptūĚĚé1superscriptsubscriptūĚúĒ10subscriptūĚúĒ11subscriptūĚúĒ12subscriptūĚúĒ13ūĚĎásubscriptūĚĚé2superscriptsubscriptūĚúĒ20subscriptūĚúĒ21subscriptūĚúĒ22subscriptūĚúĒ23ūĚĎá\bm{\omega}_{1}=(\omega_{10},\omega_{11},\omega_{12},\omega_{13})^{T},\qquad\bm{\omega}_{2}=(\omega_{20},\omega_{21},\omega_{22},\omega_{23})^{T}.

Then we put

ūĚĚé=(ūĚĚé1ūĚĚé2),W=(U1‚ÄčV11U1‚ÄčV12U2‚ÄčV21U2‚ÄčV22),formulae-sequenceūĚĚésubscriptūĚĚé1subscriptūĚĚé2ūĚĎäsubscriptūĚĎą1subscriptūĚĎČ11subscriptūĚĎą1subscriptūĚĎČ12subscriptūĚĎą2subscriptūĚĎČ21subscriptūĚĎą2subscriptūĚĎČ22\bm{\omega}=\left(\begin{array}[]{c}\bm{\omega}_{1}\\ \bm{\omega}_{2}\end{array}\right),\qquad W=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}U_{1}V_{11}&U_{1}V_{12}\\ U_{2}V_{21}&U_{2}V_{22}\end{array}\right),

where the matrices U1subscriptūĚĎą1U_{1} and U2subscriptūĚĎą2U_{2} are defined as

U1subscriptūĚĎą1\displaystyle U_{1} =(111őľőľ+őĹ12őľ+őĹőľ+2‚ÄčőĹőľőľ+ŌĄőĹőĹ+ŌĄőľ+őĹőľ+őĹ+ŌĄőľőľ+őĹ+ŌĄőĹ2‚ÄčőĹ+ŌĄőľ+őĹőľ+2‚ÄčőĹ+ŌĄ),absent111ūĚúáūĚúáūĚúą12ūĚúáūĚúąūĚúá2ūĚúąūĚúáūĚúáūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúáūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúáūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúą2ūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúá2ūĚúąūĚúŹ\displaystyle=\left(\begin{array}[]{ccc}1&1&1\\ \frac{\mu}{\mu+\nu}&\frac{1}{2}&\frac{\mu+\nu}{\mu+2\nu}\\ \frac{\mu}{\mu+\tau}&\frac{\nu}{\nu+\tau}&\frac{\mu+\nu}{\mu+\nu+\tau}\\ \frac{\mu}{\mu+\nu+\tau}&\frac{\nu}{2\nu+\tau}&\frac{\mu+\nu}{\mu+2\nu+\tau}\end{array}\right),
U2subscriptūĚĎą2\displaystyle U_{2} =(111ŌÉőľ+ŌÉŌĄőľ+ŌĄŌÉ+ŌĄőľ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ12ŌĄŌÉ+ŌĄŌÉ+ŌĄ2‚ÄčŌÉ+ŌĄŌÉőľ+2‚ÄčŌÉŌĄőľ+ŌÉ+ŌĄŌÉ+ŌĄőľ+2‚ÄčŌÉ+ŌĄ).absent111ūĚúéūĚúáūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúŹūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúéūĚúŹ12ūĚúŹūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúéūĚúŹ2ūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúéūĚúá2ūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúá2ūĚúéūĚúŹ\displaystyle=\left(\begin{array}[]{ccc}1&1&1\\ \frac{\sigma}{\mu+\sigma}&\frac{\tau}{\mu+\tau}&\frac{\sigma+\tau}{\mu+\sigma+\tau}\\ \frac{1}{2}&\frac{\tau}{\sigma+\tau}&\frac{\sigma+\tau}{2\sigma+\tau}\\ \frac{\sigma}{\mu+2\sigma}&\frac{\tau}{\mu+\sigma+\tau}&\frac{\sigma+\tau}{\mu+2\sigma+\tau}\end{array}\right).

Furthermore, the matrices V11subscriptūĚĎČ11V_{11} and V12subscriptūĚĎČ12V_{12} are defined as

V11subscriptūĚĎČ11\displaystyle V_{11} =(ŌÉőľ+ŌÉ0‚ąíőľ‚ÄčŌÉ(őľ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)00ŌÉőĹ+ŌÉ0‚ąíőĹ‚ÄčŌÉ(őĹ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)0‚ąíŌÉőľ+őĹ+ŌÉ0ŌÉ‚Äč(őľ+őĹ)(őľ+őĹ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)),absentūĚúéūĚúáūĚúé0ūĚúáūĚúéūĚúáūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúéūĚúŹ00ūĚúéūĚúąūĚúé0ūĚúąūĚúéūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹ0ūĚúéūĚúáūĚúąūĚúé0ūĚúéūĚúáūĚúąūĚúáūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹ\displaystyle=\left(\begin{array}[]{cccc}\frac{\sigma}{\mu+\sigma}&0&-\frac{\mu\sigma}{(\mu+\tau)(\mu+\sigma+\tau)}&0\\ 0&\frac{\sigma}{\nu+\sigma}&0&-\frac{\nu\sigma}{(\nu+\tau)(\nu+\sigma+\tau)}\\ 0&-\frac{\sigma}{\mu+\nu+\sigma}&0&\frac{\sigma(\mu+\nu)}{(\mu+\nu+\tau)(\mu+\nu+\sigma+\tau)}\end{array}\right),
V12subscriptūĚĎČ12\displaystyle V_{12} =(0ŌĄőľ+ŌĄ0‚ąíőľ‚ÄčŌĄ(őľ+ŌÉ)‚Äč(őľ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)ŌĄőĹ+ŌĄ0‚ąíőĹ‚ÄčŌĄ(őĹ+ŌÉ)‚Äč(őĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)00‚ąíŌĄőľ+őĹ+ŌĄ0ŌĄ‚Äč(őľ+őĹ)(őľ+őĹ+ŌÉ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)),absent0ūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúŹ0ūĚúáūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúéūĚúáūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúŹ0ūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúéūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹ00ūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúŹ0ūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúáūĚúąūĚúéūĚúáūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹ\displaystyle=\left(\begin{array}[]{cccc}0&\frac{\tau}{\mu+\tau}&0&-\frac{\mu\tau}{(\mu+\sigma)(\mu+\sigma+\tau)}\\ \frac{\tau}{\nu+\tau}&0&-\frac{\nu\tau}{(\nu+\sigma)(\nu+\sigma+\tau)}&0\\ 0&-\frac{\tau}{\mu+\nu+\tau}&0&\frac{\tau(\mu+\nu)}{(\mu+\nu+\sigma)(\mu+\nu+\sigma+\tau)}\end{array}\right),

whereas V21subscriptūĚĎČ21V_{21} and V22subscriptūĚĎČ22V_{22} take the form

V21subscriptūĚĎČ21\displaystyle V_{21} =(őľőľ+ŌÉ‚ąíőľ‚ÄčŌÉ(őĹ+ŌÉ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌÉ)0000őľőľ+ŌĄ‚ąíőľ‚ÄčŌĄ(őĹ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌĄ)00‚ąíőľőľ+ŌÉ+ŌĄőľ‚Äč(ŌÉ+ŌĄ)(őĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)),absentūĚúáūĚúáūĚúéūĚúáūĚúéūĚúąūĚúéūĚúáūĚúąūĚúé0000ūĚúáūĚúáūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúŹ00ūĚúáūĚúáūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹ\displaystyle=\left(\begin{array}[]{cccc}\frac{\mu}{\mu+\sigma}&-\frac{\mu\sigma}{(\nu+\sigma)(\mu+\nu+\sigma)}&0&0\\ 0&0&\frac{\mu}{\mu+\tau}&-\frac{\mu\tau}{(\nu+\tau)(\mu+\nu+\tau)}\\ 0&0&-\frac{\mu}{\mu+\sigma+\tau}&\frac{\mu(\sigma+\tau)}{(\nu+\sigma+\tau)(\mu+\nu+\sigma+\tau)}\end{array}\right),
V22subscriptūĚĎČ22\displaystyle V_{22} =(00őĹőĹ+ŌÉ‚ąíőĹ‚ÄčŌÉ(őľ+ŌÉ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌÉ)őĹőĹ+ŌĄ‚ąíőĹ‚ÄčŌĄ(őľ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌĄ)0000‚ąíőĹőĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄőĹ‚Äč(ŌÉ+ŌĄ)(őľ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)).absent00ūĚúąūĚúąūĚúéūĚúąūĚúéūĚúáūĚúéūĚúáūĚúąūĚúéūĚúąūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúŹ0000ūĚúąūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹ\displaystyle=\left(\begin{array}[]{cccc}0&0&\frac{\nu}{\nu+\sigma}&-\frac{\nu\sigma}{(\mu+\sigma)(\mu+\nu+\sigma)}\\ \frac{\nu}{\nu+\tau}&-\frac{\nu\tau}{(\mu+\tau)(\mu+\nu+\tau)}&0&0\\ 0&0&-\frac{\nu}{\nu+\sigma+\tau}&\frac{\nu(\sigma+\tau)}{(\mu+\sigma+\tau)(\mu+\nu+\sigma+\tau)}\end{array}\right).

Suppose the vector ūĚĚéūĚĚé\bm{\omega} is the solution of the system

(I‚ąíW)‚ÄčūĚĚéūĚźľūĚĎäūĚĚé\displaystyle(I-W)\bm{\omega} =ūĚüé,absent0\displaystyle=\bm{0},
ŌČ10+ŌČ20subscriptūĚúĒ10subscriptūĚúĒ20\displaystyle\omega_{10}+\omega_{20} =1,absent1\displaystyle=1,

where IūĚźľI represents the identity matrix, ūĚüé0\bm{0} is zero vector.

The mean cycle time is then given by

őĽ=ūĚíí1T‚ÄčūĚĚé1+ūĚíí2T‚ÄčūĚĚé2,ūĚúÜsuperscriptsubscriptūĚíí1ūĚĎásubscriptūĚĚé1superscriptsubscriptūĚíí2ūĚĎásubscriptūĚĚé2\lambda=\bm{q}_{1}^{T}\bm{\omega}_{1}+\bm{q}_{2}^{T}\bm{\omega}_{2},

where ūĚíí1subscriptūĚíí1\bm{q}_{1} and ūĚíí2subscriptūĚíí2\bm{q}_{2} are vectors such that

ūĚíí1subscriptūĚíí1\displaystyle\bm{q}_{1} =(őľ2+őľ‚ÄčŌÉ+ŌÉ2őľ‚ÄčŌÉ‚Äč(őľ+ŌÉ)őľ‚ÄčŌÉ‚Äč(őľ+2‚ÄčőĹ+ŌÉ)őĹ‚Äč(őľ+őĹ)‚Äč(őĹ+ŌÉ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌÉ)őľ‚ÄčŌÉ‚Äč(őľ+ŌÉ+2‚ÄčŌĄ)ŌĄ‚Äč(őľ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(ŌÉ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)‚ąíőľ‚ÄčŌÉ‚Äč(őľ+2‚ÄčőĹ+2‚ÄčŌĄ+ŌÉ)(őĹ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)),absentsuperscriptūĚúá2ūĚúáūĚúésuperscriptūĚúé2ūĚúáūĚúéūĚúáūĚúémissing-subexpressionūĚúáūĚúéūĚúá2ūĚúąūĚúéūĚúąūĚúáūĚúąūĚúąūĚúéūĚúáūĚúąūĚúémissing-subexpressionūĚúáūĚúéūĚúáūĚúé2ūĚúŹūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúŹūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúéūĚúŹmissing-subexpressionūĚúáūĚúéūĚúá2ūĚúą2ūĚúŹūĚúéūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹmissing-subexpression\displaystyle=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\frac{\mu^{2}+\mu\sigma+\sigma^{2}}{\mu\sigma(\mu+\sigma)}\\ \frac{\mu\sigma(\mu+2\nu+\sigma)}{\nu(\mu+\nu)(\nu+\sigma)(\mu+\nu+\sigma)}\\ \frac{\mu\sigma(\mu+\sigma+2\tau)}{\tau(\mu+\tau)(\sigma+\tau)(\mu+\sigma+\tau)}\\ -\frac{\mu\sigma(\mu+2\nu+2\tau+\sigma)}{(\nu+\tau)(\mu+\nu+\tau)(\nu+\sigma+\tau)(\mu+\nu+\sigma+\tau)}\end{array}\right),
ūĚíí2subscriptūĚíí2\displaystyle\bm{q}_{2} =(őĹ2+őĹ‚ÄčŌĄ+ŌĄ2őĹ‚ÄčŌĄ‚Äč(őĹ+ŌĄ)őĹ‚ÄčŌĄ‚Äč(2‚Äčőľ+őĹ+ŌĄ)őľ‚Äč(őľ+őĹ)‚Äč(őľ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌĄ)őĹ‚ÄčŌĄ‚Äč(őĹ+2‚ÄčŌÉ+ŌĄ)ŌÉ‚Äč(őĹ+ŌÉ)‚Äč(ŌÉ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)‚ąíőĹ‚ÄčŌĄ‚Äč(2‚Äčőľ+őĹ+2‚ÄčŌÉ+ŌĄ)(őľ+ŌÉ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌÉ)‚Äč(őľ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ+őĹ+ŌÉ+ŌĄ)).absentsuperscriptūĚúą2ūĚúąūĚúŹsuperscriptūĚúŹ2ūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúŹ2ūĚúáūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúáūĚúąūĚúáūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúŹūĚúą2ūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúéūĚúąūĚúéūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúąūĚúŹ2ūĚúáūĚúą2ūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúéūĚúáūĚúąūĚúéūĚúáūĚúéūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúąūĚúéūĚúŹ\displaystyle=\left(\begin{array}[]{c}\frac{\nu^{2}+\nu\tau+\tau^{2}}{\nu\tau(\nu+\tau)}\\ \frac{\nu\tau(2\mu+\nu+\tau)}{\mu(\mu+\nu)(\mu+\tau)(\mu+\nu+\tau)}\\ \frac{\nu\tau(\nu+2\sigma+\tau)}{\sigma(\nu+\sigma)(\sigma+\tau)(\nu+\sigma+\tau)}\\ -\frac{\nu\tau(2\mu+\nu+2\sigma+\tau)}{(\mu+\sigma)(\mu+\nu+\sigma)(\mu+\sigma+\tau)(\mu+\nu+\sigma+\tau)}\end{array}\right).

3.2 Systems With Matrices With Zero Entries

Suppose the time to perform certain operations in a system (e.g., production of output at a center or transmission of messages between stations) is sufficiently short as compared to that of the other operations. Under this assumption, one can usually set the duration of the operations equal to zero with negligible loss of accuracy, and then consider equation (4) with a matrix that includes one or more zero entries.

The solution method in this case is based on construction of a sequence of probability distribution functions. The convergence of the sequence is examined and the limiting distribution is derived as the solution of an integral equation. The mean cycle time is then evaluated as the expected value of a r.v. determined through the limiting distribution function. The results obtained are as follows.

3.2.1 Matrices With Zero Off-Diagonal Entries

The system state transition matrix together with the related result take the form

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪk00őīk),őĽ=őľ4+őľ3‚ÄčŌĄ+őľ2‚ÄčŌĄ2+őľ‚ÄčŌĄ3+ŌĄ4őľ‚ÄčŌĄ‚Äč(őľ+ŌĄ)‚Äč(őľ2+ŌĄ2).formulae-sequenceūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė00subscriptūĚõŅūĚĎėūĚúÜsuperscriptūĚúá4superscriptūĚúá3ūĚúŹsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúŹ2ūĚúásuperscriptūĚúŹ3superscriptūĚúŹ4ūĚúáūĚúŹūĚúáūĚúŹsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúŹ2A(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&0\\ 0&\delta_{k}\end{array}\right),\quad\lambda=\frac{\mu^{4}+\mu^{3}\tau+\mu^{2}\tau^{2}+\mu\tau^{3}+\tau^{4}}{\mu\tau(\mu+\tau)(\mu^{2}+\tau^{2})}.

With ŌĄ=őľ=1ūĚúŹūĚúá1\tau=\mu=1 we have the mean cycle time őĽ=1.25ūĚúÜ1.25\lambda=1.25 which coincides with that in [8].

3.2.2 Matrices With Zero Diagonal

In this case, the matrix and the mean cycle time are represented as

A‚Äč(k)=(0ő≤kő≥k0),őĽ=4‚ÄčőĹ2+7‚ÄčőĹ‚ÄčŌÉ+4‚ÄčŌÉ26‚ÄčőĹ‚ÄčŌÉ‚Äč(őĹ+ŌÉ).formulae-sequenceūĚźīūĚĎė0subscriptūĚõĹūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎė0ūĚúÜ4superscriptūĚúą27ūĚúąūĚúé4superscriptūĚúé26ūĚúąūĚúéūĚúąūĚúéA(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}0&\beta_{k}\\ \gamma_{k}&0\end{array}\right),\quad\lambda=\frac{4\nu^{2}+7\nu\sigma+4\sigma^{2}}{6\nu\sigma(\nu+\sigma)}.

3.2.3 Matrices With Zero Row or Column

Provided that the second row in the matrix has only zero entries, we arrive at

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪkő≤k00),őĽ=2‚Äčőľ4+7‚Äčőľ3‚ÄčőĹ+10‚Äčőľ2‚ÄčőĹ2+11‚Äčőľ‚ÄčőĹ3+4‚ÄčőĹ4őľ‚ÄčőĹ‚Äč(őľ+őĹ)2‚Äč(3‚Äčőľ+4‚ÄčőĹ).formulae-sequenceūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõĹūĚĎė00ūĚúÜ2superscriptūĚúá47superscriptūĚúá3ūĚúą10superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúą211ūĚúásuperscriptūĚúą34superscriptūĚúą4ūĚúáūĚúąsuperscriptūĚúáūĚúą23ūĚúá4ūĚúąA(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&\beta_{k}\\ 0&0\end{array}\right),\qquad\lambda=\frac{2\mu^{4}+7\mu^{3}\nu+10\mu^{2}\nu^{2}+11\mu\nu^{3}+4\nu^{4}}{\mu\nu(\mu+\nu)^{2}(3\mu+4\nu)}.

Considering that őĽūĚúÜ\lambda is invariant to transposition of the matrix, we immediately get the solution when the entries of the second column are zero

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪk0ő≥k0),őĽ=2‚Äčőľ4+7‚Äčőľ3‚ÄčŌÉ+10‚Äčőľ2‚ÄčŌÉ2+11‚Äčőľ‚ÄčŌÉ3+4‚ÄčŌÉ4őľ‚ÄčŌÉ‚Äč(őľ+ŌÉ)2‚Äč(3‚Äčőľ+4‚ÄčŌÉ).formulae-sequenceūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė0subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎė0ūĚúÜ2superscriptūĚúá47superscriptūĚúá3ūĚúé10superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúé211ūĚúásuperscriptūĚúé34superscriptūĚúé4ūĚúáūĚúésuperscriptūĚúáūĚúé23ūĚúá4ūĚúéA(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&0\\ \gamma_{k}&0\end{array}\right),\qquad\lambda=\frac{2\mu^{4}+7\mu^{3}\sigma+10\mu^{2}\sigma^{2}+11\mu\sigma^{3}+4\sigma^{4}}{\mu\sigma(\mu+\sigma)^{2}(3\mu+4\sigma)}.

3.2.4 Matrices With One Zero Diagonal Entry

Consider a system with the state transition matrix

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪkő≤kő≥k0).ūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõĹūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎė0A(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&\beta_{k}\\ \gamma_{k}&0\end{array}\right).

Although evaluation of the mean cycle time for this system in the general case leads to rather cumbersome algebraic manipulations, there are two main particular cases that offer results in a relatively compact form. Under the condition that ŌÉ=őľūĚúéūĚúá\sigma=\mu, we have

őĽ=48‚Äčőľ5+238‚Äčőľ4‚ÄčőĹ+495‚Äčőľ3‚ÄčőĹ2+581‚Äčőľ2‚ÄčőĹ3+326‚Äčőľ‚ÄčőĹ4+68‚ÄčőĹ52‚Äčőľ‚ÄčőĹ‚Äč(36‚Äčőľ4+147‚Äčőľ3‚ÄčőĹ+215‚Äčőľ2‚ÄčőĹ2+130‚Äčőľ‚ÄčőĹ3+28‚ÄčőĹ4).ūĚúÜ48superscriptūĚúá5238superscriptūĚúá4ūĚúą495superscriptūĚúá3superscriptūĚúą2581superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúą3326ūĚúásuperscriptūĚúą468superscriptūĚúą52ūĚúáūĚúą36superscriptūĚúá4147superscriptūĚúá3ūĚúą215superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúą2130ūĚúásuperscriptūĚúą328superscriptūĚúą4\lambda=\frac{48\mu^{5}+238\mu^{4}\nu+495\mu^{3}\nu^{2}+581\mu^{2}\nu^{3}+326\mu\nu^{4}+68\nu^{5}}{2\mu\nu(36\mu^{4}+147\mu^{3}\nu+215\mu^{2}\nu^{2}+130\mu\nu^{3}+28\nu^{4})}.

Provided that ŌÉ=őĹūĚúéūĚúą\sigma=\nu, the mean cycle time is given by

őĽ=P‚Äč(őľ,őĹ)/Q‚Äč(őľ,őĹ),ūĚúÜūĚĎÉūĚúáūĚúąūĚĎĄūĚúáūĚúą\lambda=P(\mu,\nu)/Q(\mu,\nu),

where

P‚Äč(őľ,őĹ)ūĚĎÉūĚúáūĚúą\displaystyle P(\mu,\nu) =15‚Äčőľ8+152‚Äčőľ7‚ÄčőĹ+624‚Äčőľ6‚ÄčőĹ2+1382‚Äčőľ5‚ÄčőĹ3+1838‚Äčőľ4‚ÄčőĹ4absent15superscriptūĚúá8152superscriptūĚúá7ūĚúą624superscriptūĚúá6superscriptūĚúą21382superscriptūĚúá5superscriptūĚúą31838superscriptūĚúá4superscriptūĚúą4\displaystyle=15\mu^{8}+152\mu^{7}\nu+624\mu^{6}\nu^{2}+1382\mu^{5}\nu^{3}+1838\mu^{4}\nu^{4}
+1592‚Äčőľ3‚ÄčőĹ5+973‚Äčőľ2‚ÄčőĹ6+384‚Äčőľ‚ÄčőĹ7+64‚ÄčőĹ8,1592superscriptūĚúá3superscriptūĚúą5973superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúą6384ūĚúásuperscriptūĚúą764superscriptūĚúą8\displaystyle\phantom{=}+1592\mu^{3}\nu^{5}+973\mu^{2}\nu^{6}+384\mu\nu^{7}+64\nu^{8},
Q‚Äč(őľ,őĹ)ūĚĎĄūĚúáūĚúą\displaystyle Q(\mu,\nu) =őľőĹ(őľ+őĹ)2(12őľ5+97őľ4őĹ+286őľ3őĹ2+397őľ2őĹ3\displaystyle=\mu\nu(\mu+\nu)^{2}(12\mu^{5}+97\mu^{4}\nu+286\mu^{3}\nu^{2}+397\mu^{2}\nu^{3}
+256őľőĹ4+64őĹ5).\displaystyle\phantom{=}+256\mu\nu^{4}+64\nu^{5}).

3.2.5 Matrices With One Zero Off-Diagonal Entry

Suppose that there is a system with a state transition matrix defined as

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪkő≤k0őīk).ūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõĹūĚĎė0subscriptūĚõŅūĚĎėA(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&\beta_{k}\\ 0&\delta_{k}\end{array}\right).

Consider two particular cases. With the condition őĹ=őľūĚúąūĚúá\nu=\mu, we have

őĽ=P‚Äč(őľ,ŌĄ)/Q‚Äč(őľ,ŌĄ),ūĚúÜūĚĎÉūĚúáūĚúŹūĚĎĄūĚúáūĚúŹ\lambda=P(\mu,\tau)/Q(\mu,\tau),

where

P‚Äč(őľ,ŌĄ)ūĚĎÉūĚúáūĚúŹ\displaystyle P(\mu,\tau) =288‚Äčőľ8+1048‚Äčőľ7‚ÄčŌĄ+1936‚Äčőľ6‚ÄčŌĄ2+2688‚Äčőľ5‚ÄčŌĄ3absent288superscriptūĚúá81048superscriptūĚúá7ūĚúŹ1936superscriptūĚúá6superscriptūĚúŹ22688superscriptūĚúá5superscriptūĚúŹ3\displaystyle=288\mu^{8}+1048\mu^{7}\tau+1936\mu^{6}\tau^{2}+2688\mu^{5}\tau^{3}
+3012‚Äčőľ4‚ÄčŌĄ4+2226‚Äčőľ3‚ÄčŌĄ5+941‚Äčőľ2‚ÄčŌĄ6+204‚Äčőľ‚ÄčŌĄ7+17‚ÄčŌĄ8,3012superscriptūĚúá4superscriptūĚúŹ42226superscriptūĚúá3superscriptūĚúŹ5941superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúŹ6204ūĚúásuperscriptūĚúŹ717superscriptūĚúŹ8\displaystyle\phantom{=}+3012\mu^{4}\tau^{4}+2226\mu^{3}\tau^{5}+941\mu^{2}\tau^{6}+204\mu\tau^{7}+17\tau^{8},
Q‚Äč(őľ,ŌĄ)ūĚĎĄūĚúáūĚúŹ\displaystyle Q(\mu,\tau) =2őľŌĄ(144őľ7+524őľ6ŌĄ+968őľ5ŌĄ2+1200őľ4ŌĄ3\displaystyle=2\mu\tau(144\mu^{7}+524\mu^{6}\tau+968\mu^{5}\tau^{2}+1200\mu^{4}\tau^{3}
+910őľ3ŌĄ4+387őľ2ŌĄ5+84őľŌĄ6+7ŌĄ7).\displaystyle\phantom{=}+910\mu^{3}\tau^{4}+387\mu^{2}\tau^{5}+84\mu\tau^{6}+7\tau^{7}).

Provided that ŌĄ=őľūĚúŹūĚúá\tau=\mu, the solution takes the form

őĽ=P‚Äč(őľ,őĹ)/Q‚Äč(őľ,őĹ),ūĚúÜūĚĎÉūĚúáūĚúąūĚĎĄūĚúáūĚúą\lambda=P(\mu,\nu)/Q(\mu,\nu),

where

P‚Äč(őľ,őĹ)ūĚĎÉūĚúáūĚúą\displaystyle P(\mu,\nu) =256‚Äčőľ10+2112‚Äčőľ9‚ÄčőĹ+8044‚Äčőľ8‚ÄčőĹ2+19355‚Äčőľ7‚ÄčőĹ3absent256superscriptūĚúá102112superscriptūĚúá9ūĚúą8044superscriptūĚúá8superscriptūĚúą219355superscriptūĚúá7superscriptūĚúą3\displaystyle=256\mu^{10}+2112\mu^{9}\nu+8044\mu^{8}\nu^{2}+19355\mu^{7}\nu^{3}
+32167‚Äčőľ6‚ÄčőĹ4+36887‚Äčőľ5‚ÄčőĹ5+28709‚Äčőľ4‚ÄčőĹ632167superscriptūĚúá6superscriptūĚúą436887superscriptūĚúá5superscriptūĚúą528709superscriptūĚúá4superscriptūĚúą6\displaystyle\phantom{=}+32167\mu^{6}\nu^{4}+36887\mu^{5}\nu^{5}+28709\mu^{4}\nu^{6}
+14854‚Äčőľ3‚ÄčőĹ7+4912‚Äčőľ2‚ÄčőĹ8+944‚Äčőľ‚ÄčőĹ9+80‚ÄčőĹ10,14854superscriptūĚúá3superscriptūĚúą74912superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúą8944ūĚúásuperscriptūĚúą980superscriptūĚúą10\displaystyle\phantom{=}+14854\mu^{3}\nu^{7}+4912\mu^{2}\nu^{8}+944\mu\nu^{9}+80\nu^{10},
Q‚Äč(őľ,őĹ)ūĚĎĄūĚúáūĚúą\displaystyle Q(\mu,\nu) =2őľőĹ(őľ+őĹ)(192őľ8+1344őľ7őĹ+4047őľ6őĹ2\displaystyle=2\mu\nu(\mu+\nu)(192\mu^{8}+1344\mu^{7}\nu+4047\mu^{6}\nu^{2}
+6770‚Äčőľ5‚ÄčőĹ3+6799‚Äčőľ4‚ÄčőĹ4+4216‚Äčőľ3‚ÄčőĹ5+1600‚Äčőľ2‚ÄčőĹ66770superscriptūĚúá5superscriptūĚúą36799superscriptūĚúá4superscriptūĚúą44216superscriptūĚúá3superscriptūĚúą51600superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúą6\displaystyle\phantom{=}+6770\mu^{5}\nu^{3}+6799\mu^{4}\nu^{4}+4216\mu^{3}\nu^{5}+1600\mu^{2}\nu^{6}
+344őľőĹ7+32őĹ8).\displaystyle\phantom{=}+344\mu\nu^{7}+32\nu^{8}).

4 Results for Matrices with Arbitrary Constant Entries

Now we present new results that extend some of the solutions in the previous section to the case of matrices with arbitrary nonnegative constants in place of one or more entries. The results allows one to take account of various possible settings for activity times in actual systems from a random time to constant and zero time.

4.1 Matrices With One Random Entry

We examine systems with matrices that have one random entry, whereas the other entries can be arbitrary nonnegative and zero constants. To evaluate the mean cycle time, we apply the same technique as that developed for matrices with zero entries and outlined in the previous section.

The random entry in system matrices is always assumed to have exponential distribution except for one case of a matrix with zero row. In the later case, the particular form of the matrix enables one to obtain a solution that does not rely on exponential distribution assumptions.

4.1.1 Matrices With Zero Off-Diagonal Entries

We start with a system that has a matrix

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪk00c),ūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė00ūĚĎźA(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&0\\ 0&c\end{array}\right),

where cūĚĎźc is a nonnegative constant, őĪksubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė\alpha_{k} is a r.v. that has exponential distribution with parameter őľūĚúá\mu.

The solution procedure that is described in detail in Appendix¬†A gives the result

őĽ=c+2‚Äče‚ąí3‚Äčőľ‚Äčcőľ‚Äč(2‚ąí4‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚Äče‚ąíőľ‚Äčc+őľ2‚Äčc2‚Äče‚ąí2‚Äčőľ‚Äčc).ūĚúÜūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎí3ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚúá24ūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎí2ūĚúáūĚĎź\lambda=c+\frac{2e^{-3\mu c}}{\mu(2-4\mu ce^{-\mu c}+\mu^{2}c^{2}e^{-2\mu c})}.

Figures¬†1 and 2 show graphs of őĽūĚúÜ\lambda as functions of cūĚĎźc and őľūĚúá\mu.

4.1.2 Matrices With Zero Row or Column

With similar computations as above, we get the solutions

A‚Äč(k)=(cő≤k00),őĽ=c+2‚Äče‚ąí2‚ÄčőĹ‚ÄčcőĹ‚Äč(2+e‚ąí2‚ÄčőĹ‚Äčc),formulae-sequenceūĚźīūĚĎėūĚĎźsubscriptūĚõĹūĚĎė00ūĚúÜūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎí2ūĚúąūĚĎźūĚúą2superscriptūĚĎí2ūĚúąūĚĎźA(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}c&\beta_{k}\\ 0&0\end{array}\right),\quad\lambda=c+\frac{2e^{-2\nu c}}{\nu(2+e^{-2\nu c})},

and

A‚Äč(k)=(c0ő≥k0),őĽ=c+2‚Äče‚ąí2‚ÄčŌÉ‚ÄčcŌÉ‚Äč(2+e‚ąí2‚ÄčŌÉ‚Äčc).formulae-sequenceūĚźīūĚĎėūĚĎź0subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎė0ūĚúÜūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎí2ūĚúéūĚĎźūĚúé2superscriptūĚĎí2ūĚúéūĚĎźA(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}c&0\\ \gamma_{k}&0\end{array}\right),\quad\lambda=c+\frac{2e^{-2\sigma c}}{\sigma(2+e^{-2\sigma c})}.

Graphical representation of őĽūĚúÜ\lambda with respect to cūĚĎźc and őľūĚúá\mu is of much the same shape as that of respective graphs depicted in Figures¬†1 and 2.

Now we present a system that admits a solution under more fairly conditions and in a quite general form. Consider a system with a matrix

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪkc00),ūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎź00A(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&c\\ 0&0\end{array}\right),

and assume cūĚĎźc to be a nonnegative constant and őĪksubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė\alpha_{k} to be a nonnegative r.v. that has a distribution function F‚Äč(t)ūĚźĻūĚĎ°F(t) with finite mean aūĚĎéa.

As it is shown in Appendix¬†A.2, the mean cycle time for the system is given by

őĽ=a+‚ąę0cF‚Äč(t)‚ÄčF‚Äč(c‚ąít)‚Äč(1‚ąíF‚Äč(t))1‚ąíF‚Äč(t)‚ÄčF‚Äč(c‚ąít)‚ÄčūĚĎĎt.ūĚúÜūĚĎésuperscriptsubscript0ūĚĎźūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°differential-dūĚĎ°\lambda=a+\int_{0}^{c}\frac{F(t)F(c-t)(1-F(t))}{1-F(t)F(c-t)}dt.

Specifically, in the case of the exponential distribution function

F‚Äč(t)=max‚Ā°(0,1‚ąíe‚ąíőľ‚Äčt),ūĚźĻūĚĎ°01superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎ°F(t)=\max(0,1-e^{-\mu t}),

we have a=1/őľūĚĎé1ūĚúáa=1/\mu. Calculation of the integral gives

őĽ=c2+e‚ąíőľ‚Äčcőľ+3‚Äčarctan‚Ā°4‚Äčeőľ‚Äčc‚ąí1‚ąíŌÄőľ‚Äč4‚Äčeőľ‚Äčc‚ąí1.ūĚúÜūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎźūĚúá34superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎź1ūĚúčūĚúá4superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎź1\lambda=\frac{c}{2}+\frac{e^{-\mu c}}{\mu}+\frac{3\arctan\sqrt{4e^{\mu c}-1}-\pi}{\mu\sqrt{4e^{\mu c}-1}}.

A graphical illustration is given in Figures¬†3 and 4.

4.1.3 Matrices With Three Constant Entries

For a system with the symmetric matrix

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪkccc),ūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎźūĚĎźūĚĎźA(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&c\\ c&c\end{array}\right),

a solution obtained in [13] takes the form

őĽ=c+2‚Äče‚ąíőľ‚Äčcőľ‚Äč(2+e‚ąíőľ‚Äčc‚ąí2‚Äče‚ąí2‚Äčőľ‚Äčc+e‚ąí3‚Äčőľ‚Äčc).ūĚúÜūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎźūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎí2ūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚĎí3ūĚúáūĚĎź\lambda=c+\frac{2e^{-\mu c}}{\mu(2+e^{-\mu c}-2e^{-2\mu c}+e^{-3\mu c})}.

References

  • [1] V.¬†N. Kolokoltsov and V.¬†P. Maslov, Idempotent Analysis and Its Applications, vol.¬†401 of Mathematics and Its Applications. Kluwer Academic Publishers, Dordrecht, 1997.
  • [2] J.¬†S. Golan, Semirings and Affine Equations Over Them: Theory and Applications, vol.¬†556 of Mathematics and Its Applications. Springer, New York, 2003.
  • [3] B.¬†Heidergott, G.¬†J. Olsder, and J.¬†van¬†der Woude, Max-Plus at Work: Modeling and Analysis of Synchronized Systems. Princeton Series in Applied Mathematics. Princeton Univ. Press, Princeton, NJ, 2006.
  • [4] B.¬†F. Heidergott, Max-Plus Linear Stochastic Systems and Perturbation Analysis. The International Series on Discrete Event Dynamic Systems, Vol. 15. Springer, New York, 2007. http://www.springerlink.com/content/978-0-387-35206-0.
  • [5] N.¬†K. Krivulin, ‚ÄúEvaluation of lyapunov exponent in generalized linear dynamical models of queueing networks,‚ÄĚ in Proceedings MATHMOD 2009 Vienna ‚Äď Full Papers CD Volume, I.¬†Troch and F.¬†Breitenecker, eds., pp.¬†706‚Äď717, Vienna University of Technology. ARGESIM, Vienna, February, 2009.
  • [6] J.¬†A.¬†C. Resing, R.¬†E. De¬†Vries, G.¬†Hooghiemstra, M.¬†S. Keane, and G.¬†J. Olsder, ‚ÄúAsymptotic behavior of random discrete event systems,‚ÄĚ Stochastic Process. Appl. 36 no.¬†2, (December, 1990) 195‚Äď216.
  • [7] G.¬†J. Olsder, J.¬†A.¬†C. Resing, R.¬†De¬†Vries, M.¬†S. Keane, and G.¬†Hooghiemstra, ‚ÄúDiscrete event systems with stochastic processing times,‚ÄĚ IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control 35 no.¬†3, (March, 1990) 299‚Äď302.
  • [8] A.¬†Jean-Marie, ‚ÄúAnalytical computation of lyapunov exponents in stochastic event graphs,‚ÄĚ in Performance Evaluation of Parallel and Distributed Systems. Solution Methods: Proceedings of the Third QMIPS Workshop. Part 2, O.¬†J. Boxma and G.¬†M. Koole, eds., vol.¬†106 of CWI Tracts, pp.¬†309‚Äď341. CWI, Amsterdam, 1994.
  • [9] N.¬†K. Krivulin, ‚ÄúEvaluation of the growth rate of the state vector in a second-order generalized linear stochastic system,‚ÄĚ Vestnik St. Petersburg Univ. Math. 41 no.¬†1, (March, 2008) 28‚Äď38.
  • [10] N.¬†K. Krivulin, ‚ÄúGrowth rate of the state vector in a generalized linear stochastic system with a symmetric matrix,‚ÄĚ J. Math. Sci. (N.Y.) 147 no.¬†4, (December, 2007) 6924‚Äď6928.
  • [11] N.¬†K. Krivulin, ‚ÄúCalculating the lyapunov exponent for generalized linear systems with exponentially distributed elements of the transition matrix,‚ÄĚ Vestnik St. Petersburg University: Mathematics 42 no.¬†2, (June, 2009) 95‚Äď105.
  • [12] J.¬†F.¬†C. Kingman, ‚ÄúSubadditive ergodic theory,‚ÄĚ Ann. Probab 1 no.¬†6, (December, 1973) 883‚Äď899.
  • [13] N.¬†K. Krivulin, ‚ÄúCalculating the mean growth rate of the vector of states of a stochastic system with synchronization of events,‚ÄĚ Vestnik St. Petersburg Univ. Math. 44 no.¬†1, (March, 2011) 79‚Äď86.

Appendix A Solution Procedures

A.1 A Matrix With Zero Off-Diagonal Entries

We describe evaluation of the mean cycle time for system (4) with the matrix

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪk00c),ūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė00ūĚĎźA(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&0\\ 0&c\end{array}\right),

where cūĚĎźc is a nonnegative constant, őĪksubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė\alpha_{k} is a r.v. with distribution function

FőĪ‚Äč(t)=max‚Ā°(0,1‚ąíe‚ąíőľ‚Äčt).subscriptūĚźĻūĚõľūĚĎ°01superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎ°F_{\alpha}(t)=\max(0,1-e^{-\mu t}).

Note that equations (1) can now be written as

x‚Äč(k)ūĚĎ•ūĚĎė\displaystyle x(k) =max‚Ā°(x‚Äč(k‚ąí1)+őĪk,y‚Äč(k‚ąí1)),absentūĚĎ•ūĚĎė1subscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎ¶ūĚĎė1\displaystyle=\max(x(k-1)+\alpha_{k},y(k-1)),
y‚Äč(k)ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎė\displaystyle y(k) =max‚Ā°(x‚Äč(k‚ąí1),y‚Äč(k‚ąí1)+c).absentūĚĎ•ūĚĎė1ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎė1ūĚĎź\displaystyle=\max(x(k-1),y(k-1)+c).

With new variables

X‚Äč(k)ūĚĎčūĚĎė\displaystyle X(k) =x‚Äč(k)‚ąíx‚Äč(k‚ąí1),absentūĚĎ•ūĚĎėūĚĎ•ūĚĎė1\displaystyle=x(k)-x(k-1),
Y‚Äč(k)ūĚĎĆūĚĎė\displaystyle Y(k) =y‚Äč(k)‚ąíx‚Äč(k),absentūĚĎ¶ūĚĎėūĚĎ•ūĚĎė\displaystyle=y(k)-x(k),

we arrive at the equations

X‚Äč(k)ūĚĎčūĚĎė\displaystyle X(k) =max‚Ā°(őĪk,Y‚Äč(k‚ąí1)),absentsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎĆūĚĎė1\displaystyle=\max(\alpha_{k},Y(k-1)),
Y‚Äč(k)ūĚĎĆūĚĎė\displaystyle Y(k) =max‚Ā°(0,Y‚Äč(k‚ąí1)+c)‚ąímax‚Ā°(őĪk,Y‚Äč(k‚ąí1)).absent0ūĚĎĆūĚĎė1ūĚĎźsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎĆūĚĎė1\displaystyle=\max(0,Y(k-1)+c)-\max(\alpha_{k},Y(k-1)).

It is not difficult to see that X‚Äč(k)‚Č•0ūĚĎčūĚĎė0X(k)\geq 0, Y‚Äč(k)‚Č§cūĚĎĆūĚĎėūĚĎźY(k)\leq c, and

x‚Äč(k)=X‚Äč(1)+‚čĮ+X‚Äč(k).ūĚĎ•ūĚĎėūĚĎč1‚čĮūĚĎčūĚĎėx(k)=X(1)+\cdots+X(k).

Now the mean cycle time can be represented in the form

őĽ=limk‚Üí‚ąě1k‚ÄčūĚĖ§‚Äčmax‚Ā°(x‚Äč(k),y‚Äč(k))=limk‚Üí‚ąě1k‚ÄčūĚĖ§‚Äčmax‚Ā°(0,Y‚Äč(k))+limk‚Üí‚ąě1k‚Äč‚ąĎi=1kūĚĖ§‚ÄčX‚Äč(k)=limk‚Üí‚ąě1k‚Äč‚ąĎi=1kūĚĖ§‚ÄčX‚Äč(k).ūĚúÜsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎė1ūĚĎėūĚĖ§ūĚĎ•ūĚĎėūĚĎ¶ūĚĎėsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎė1ūĚĎėūĚĖ§0ūĚĎĆūĚĎėsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎė1ūĚĎėsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĖ1ūĚĎėūĚĖ§ūĚĎčūĚĎėsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎė1ūĚĎėsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĖ1ūĚĎėūĚĖ§ūĚĎčūĚĎė\lambda=\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{1}{k}\mathsf{E}\max(x(k),y(k))\\ =\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{1}{k}\mathsf{E}\max(0,Y(k))+\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\mathsf{E}X(k)\\ =\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\mathsf{E}X(k).

Consider the distribution functions

ő¶k‚Äč(t)=ūĚĖĮ‚Äč{X‚Äč(k)<t},ő®k‚Äč(t)=ūĚĖĮ‚Äč{Y‚Äč(k)<t},formulae-sequencesubscriptő¶ūĚĎėūĚĎ°ūĚĖĮūĚĎčūĚĎėūĚĎ°subscriptő®ūĚĎėūĚĎ°ūĚĖĮūĚĎĆūĚĎėūĚĎ°\Phi_{k}(t)=\mathsf{P}\{X(k)<t\},\qquad\Psi_{k}(t)=\mathsf{P}\{Y(k)<t\},

and note that

ő¶k‚Äč(t)=ūĚĖĮ‚Äč{max‚Ā°(őĪk,Y‚Äč(k‚ąí1))<t}=FőĪ‚Äč(t)‚Äčő®k‚ąí1‚Äč(t).subscriptő¶ūĚĎėūĚĎ°ūĚĖĮsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎĆūĚĎė1ūĚĎ°subscriptūĚźĻūĚõľūĚĎ°subscriptő®ūĚĎė1ūĚĎ°\Phi_{k}(t)=\mathsf{P}\{\max(\alpha_{k},Y(k-1))<t\}=F_{\alpha}(t)\Psi_{k-1}(t).

From the law of total probability it follows that

ő®k‚Äč(t)=‚ąę0‚ąěūĚĖĮ‚Äč{Y‚Äč(k)‚Äč<t|‚ÄčőĪk=u}‚ÄčūĚĎĎFőĪ‚Äč(u).subscriptő®ūĚĎėūĚĎ°superscriptsubscript0ūĚĖĮūĚĎĆūĚĎėbraūĚĎ°subscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎĘdifferential-dsubscriptūĚźĻūĚõľūĚĎĘ\Psi_{k}(t)=\int_{0}^{\infty}\mathsf{P}\{Y(k)<t|\alpha_{k}=u\}dF_{\alpha}(u).

Evaluation of the conditional probability gives

ūĚĖĮ‚Äč{Y‚Äč(k)‚Äč<t|‚ÄčőĪk=u}=ūĚĖĮ‚Äč{max‚Ā°(0,Y‚Äč(k‚ąí1)+c)‚ąímax‚Ā°(u,Y‚Äč(k‚ąí1))<t}={0,if¬†u‚Č§‚ąít,¬†t‚Č§c;ő®k‚ąí1‚Äč(u‚ąíc+t),if¬†u>‚ąít,¬†t‚Č§c;1‚ąíő®k‚ąí1‚Äč(‚ąít),if¬†u‚Č§‚ąít,¬†t>c;1,if¬†u>‚ąít,¬†t>c.ūĚĖĮūĚĎĆūĚĎėbraūĚĎ°subscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎĘūĚĖĮ0ūĚĎĆūĚĎė1ūĚĎźūĚĎĘūĚĎĆūĚĎė1ūĚĎ°cases0if¬†u‚Č§‚ąít,¬†t‚Č§csubscriptő®ūĚĎė1ūĚĎĘūĚĎźūĚĎ°if¬†u>‚ąít,¬†t‚Č§c1subscriptő®ūĚĎė1ūĚĎ°if¬†u‚Č§‚ąít,¬†t>c1if¬†u>‚ąít,¬†t>c\mathsf{P}\{Y(k)<t|\alpha_{k}=u\}\\ =\mathsf{P}\{\max(0,Y(k-1)+c)-\max(u,Y(k-1))<t\}\\ =\begin{cases}0,&\text{if $u\leq-t$, $t\leq c$};\\ \Psi_{k-1}(u-c+t),&\text{if $u>-t$, $t\leq c$};\\ 1-\Psi_{k-1}(-t),&\text{if $u\leq-t$, $t>c$};\\ 1,&\text{if $u>-t$, $t>c$}.\end{cases}

After appropriate substitutions, we arrive at the recurrence equation

ő®k‚Äč(t)={őľ‚Äčeőľ‚Äčt‚Äč‚ąę0‚ąěő®k‚ąí1‚Äč(u‚ąíc)‚Äče‚ąíőľ‚Äču‚ÄčūĚĎĎu,if¬†t‚Č§0;őľ‚Äč‚ąę0‚ąěő®k‚ąí1‚Äč(u‚ąíc+t)‚Äče‚ąíőľ‚Äču‚ÄčūĚĎĎu,if¬†0<t‚Č§c;1,if¬†t>c.subscriptő®ūĚĎėūĚĎ°casesūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎ°superscriptsubscript0subscriptő®ūĚĎė1ūĚĎĘūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎĘdifferential-dūĚĎĘif¬†t‚Č§0ūĚúásuperscriptsubscript0subscriptő®ūĚĎė1ūĚĎĘūĚĎźūĚĎ°superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎĘdifferential-dūĚĎĘif¬†0<t‚Č§c1if¬†t>c\Psi_{k}(t)=\begin{cases}\mu e^{\mu t}\int\limits_{0}^{\infty}\Psi_{k-1}(u-c)e^{-\mu u}du,&\text{if $t\leq 0$};\\ \mu\int\limits_{0}^{\infty}\Psi_{k-1}(u-c+t)e^{-\mu u}du,&\text{if $0<t\leq c$};\\ 1,&\text{if $t>c$}.\end{cases}

Let us investigate the convergence of the sequences {ő®k}subscriptő®ūĚĎė\{\Psi_{k}\} and {ő¶k}subscriptő¶ūĚĎė\{\Phi_{k}\}. We introduce the notation

ak=őľ‚Äč‚ąę‚ąíc0ő®k‚Äč(u)‚Äče‚ąíőľ‚Äču‚ÄčūĚĎĎu,bk=őľ‚Äč‚ąę0cő®k‚Äč(u)‚Äče‚ąíőľ‚Äču‚ÄčūĚĎĎu.formulae-sequencesubscriptūĚĎéūĚĎėūĚúásuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎź0subscriptő®ūĚĎėūĚĎĘsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎĘdifferential-dūĚĎĘsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎėūĚúásuperscriptsubscript0ūĚĎźsubscriptő®ūĚĎėūĚĎĘsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎĘdifferential-dūĚĎĘa_{k}=\mu\int_{-c}^{0}\Psi_{k}(u)e^{-\mu u}du,\qquad b_{k}=\mu\int_{0}^{c}\Psi_{k}(u)e^{-\mu u}du.

With C=e‚ąíőľ‚ÄčcūĚź∂superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎźC=e^{-\mu c}, the recurrence equation takes the form

ő®k‚Äč(t)={C‚Äč(ak‚ąí1+bk‚ąí1+C)‚Äčeőľ‚Äčt,if¬†t‚Č§0;C‚Äč(c‚ąítc‚Äčak‚ąí1+bk‚ąí1+C)‚Äčeőľ‚Äčt,if¬†0<t‚Č§c;1,if¬†t>c.subscriptő®ūĚĎėūĚĎ°casesūĚź∂subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎė1subscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎė1ūĚź∂superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎ°if¬†t‚Č§0ūĚź∂ūĚĎźūĚĎ°ūĚĎźsubscriptūĚĎéūĚĎė1subscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎė1ūĚź∂superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎ°if¬†0<t‚Č§c1if¬†t>c\Psi_{k}(t)=\begin{cases}C(a_{k-1}+b_{k-1}+C)e^{\mu t},&\text{if $t\leq 0$};\\ C\left(\frac{c-t}{c}a_{k-1}+b_{k-1}+C\right)e^{\mu t},&\text{if $0<t\leq c$};\\ 1,&\text{if $t>c$}.\end{cases}

It is clear that there is a one-to-one correspondence between the sequence of functions {ő®k}subscriptő®ūĚĎė\{\Psi_{k}\} and the sequence of vectors {(ak,bk)}subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎėsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎė\{(a_{k},b_{k})\}.

The last equation together with the definitions of aksubscriptūĚĎéūĚĎėa_{k} and bksubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎėb_{k} give the recurrences

aksubscriptūĚĎéūĚĎė\displaystyle a_{k} =őľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC‚Äčak‚ąí1+őľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC‚Äčbk‚ąí1+őľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC2,absentūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎė1ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂subscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎė1ūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚź∂2\displaystyle=\mu cCa_{k-1}+\mu cCb_{k-1}+\mu cC^{2},
bksubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎė\displaystyle b_{k} =őľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC2‚Äčak‚ąí1+őľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC‚Äčbk‚ąí1+őľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC2.absentūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂2subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎė1ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂subscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎė1ūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚź∂2\displaystyle=\frac{\mu cC}{2}a_{k-1}+\mu cCb_{k-1}+\mu cC^{2}.

The iteration matrix of the recurrences has eigenvalues given by

őľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC‚Äč(1¬Ī22)=őľ‚Äčc‚Äče‚ąíőľ‚Äčc‚Äč(1¬Ī22).ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂plus-or-minus122ūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎźplus-or-minus122\mu cC\left(1\pm\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)=\mu ce^{-\mu c}\left(1\pm\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right).

Since 0‚Č§x‚Äče‚ąíx‚Č§e‚ąí10ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎí10\leq xe^{-x}\leq e^{-1} for all x‚Č•0ūĚĎ•0x\geq 0, both eigenvalues have absolute values less than one. Therefore, the recurrences produce iterations that converge to a limiting vector (a,b)ūĚĎéūĚĎŹ(a,b). The components of the vector are found from the equations

(1‚ąíőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC)‚Äča‚ąíőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC‚Äčb1ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂ūĚĎéūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂ūĚĎŹ\displaystyle(1-\mu cC)a-\mu cCb =őľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC2,absentūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚź∂2\displaystyle=\mu cC^{2},
‚ąíőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC2‚Äča+(1‚ąíőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC)‚ÄčbūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂2ūĚĎé1ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂ūĚĎŹ\displaystyle-\frac{\mu cC}{2}a+(1-\mu cC)b =őľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC2,absentūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚź∂2\displaystyle=\mu cC^{2},

and are given by

a=2‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC22‚ąí4‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC+őľ2‚Äčc2‚ÄčC2,b=(2‚ąíőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC)‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC22‚ąí4‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC+őľ2‚Äčc2‚ÄčC2.formulae-sequenceūĚĎé2ūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚź∂224ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚź∂2ūĚĎŹ2ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂ūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚź∂224ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚź∂2a=\frac{2\mu cC^{2}}{2-4\mu cC+\mu^{2}c^{2}C^{2}},\qquad b=\frac{(2-\mu cC)\mu cC^{2}}{2-4\mu cC+\mu^{2}c^{2}C^{2}}.

From the convergence of the sequence {(ak,bk)}subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎėsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎė\{(a_{k},b_{k})\} it follows that the sequence {ő®k}subscriptő®ūĚĎė\{\Psi_{k}\} converges as well. The limiting function takes the form

ő®‚Äč(t)={C‚Äč(a+b+C)‚Äčeőľ‚Äčt,if¬†t‚Č§0;C‚Äč(ac‚Äč(c‚ąít)+b+C)‚Äčeőľ‚Äčt,if¬†0<t‚Č§c;1,if¬†t>c.ő®ūĚĎ°casesūĚź∂ūĚĎéūĚĎŹūĚź∂superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎ°if¬†t‚Č§0ūĚź∂ūĚĎéūĚĎźūĚĎźūĚĎ°ūĚĎŹūĚź∂superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎ°if¬†0<t‚Č§c1if¬†t>c\Psi(t)=\begin{cases}C(a+b+C)e^{\mu t},&\text{if $t\leq 0$};\\ C(\frac{a}{c}(c-t)+b+C)e^{\mu t},&\text{if $0<t\leq c$};\\ 1,&\text{if $t>c$}.\end{cases}

Substitution of the values for aūĚĎéa and būĚĎŹb yields

ő®‚Äč(t)={2‚ÄčC22‚ąí4‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC+őľ2‚Äčc2‚ÄčC2‚Äčeőľ‚Äčt,if¬†t‚Č§0;2‚ÄčC22‚ąí4‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC+őľ2‚Äčc2‚ÄčC2‚Äč(1‚ąíőľ‚ÄčC‚Äčt)‚Äčeőľ‚Äčt,if¬†0<t‚Č§c;1,if¬†t>c.ő®ūĚĎ°cases2superscriptūĚź∂224ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚź∂2superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎ°if¬†t‚Č§02superscriptūĚź∂224ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚź∂21ūĚúáūĚź∂ūĚĎ°superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎ°if¬†0<t‚Č§c1if¬†t>c\Psi(t)=\begin{cases}\frac{2C^{2}}{2-4\mu cC+\mu^{2}c^{2}C^{2}}e^{\mu t},&\text{if $t\leq 0$};\\ \frac{2C^{2}}{2-4\mu cC+\mu^{2}c^{2}C^{2}}(1-\mu Ct)e^{\mu t},&\text{if $0<t\leq c$};\\ 1,&\text{if $t>c$}.\end{cases}

Furthermore, the sequence {ő¶k}subscriptő¶ūĚĎė\{\Phi_{k}\} also converges to a function given by

ő¶‚Äč(t)=FőĪ‚Äč(t)‚Äčő®‚Äč(t)=={0,if¬†t‚Č§0;2‚ÄčC22‚ąí4‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC+őľ2‚Äčc2‚ÄčC2‚Äč(‚ąí1+őľ‚ÄčC‚Äčt)‚Äč(1‚ąíeőľ‚Äčt),if¬†0<t‚Č§c;1‚ąíe‚ąíőľ‚Äčt,if¬†t>c.ő¶ūĚĎ°subscriptūĚźĻūĚõľūĚĎ°ő®ūĚĎ°cases0if¬†t‚Č§02superscriptūĚź∂224ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚź∂21ūĚúáūĚź∂ūĚĎ°1superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎ°if¬†0<t‚Č§c1superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎ°if¬†t>c\Phi(t)=F_{\alpha}(t)\Psi(t)=\\ =\begin{cases}0,&\text{if $t\leq 0$};\\ \frac{2C^{2}}{2-4\mu cC+\mu^{2}c^{2}C^{2}}(-1+\mu Ct)(1-e^{\mu t}),&\text{if $0<t\leq c$};\\ 1-e^{-\mu t},&\text{if $t>c$}.\end{cases}

It is easy to see that ő¶ő¶\Phi presents a distribution function of a r.v. that we denote by XūĚĎčX. We note that ūĚĖĮ‚Äč{X=c}>0ūĚĖĮūĚĎčūĚĎź0\mathsf{P}\{X=c\}>0 whereas cūĚĎźc is the only single value the variable XūĚĎčX can take with positive probability.

The convergence of the distribution functions ő¶k‚Üíő¶‚Üísubscriptő¶ūĚĎėő¶\Phi_{k}\to\Phi involves the convergence of means ūĚĖ§‚ÄčX‚Äč(k)‚ÜíūĚĖ§‚ÄčX‚ÜíūĚĖ§ūĚĎčūĚĎėūĚĖ§ūĚĎč\mathsf{E}X(k)\to\mathsf{E}X. This leads to the conclusion that

őĽ=limk‚Üí‚ąě1k‚Äč‚ąĎi=1kūĚĖ§‚ÄčX‚Äč(k)=ūĚĖ§‚ÄčX.ūĚúÜsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎė1ūĚĎėsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĖ1ūĚĎėūĚĖ§ūĚĎčūĚĎėūĚĖ§ūĚĎč\lambda=\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\mathsf{E}X(k)=\mathsf{E}X.

To evaluate the last expectation, we first find the probability

p=ūĚĖĮ‚Äč{X=c}=1‚ąí‚ąę0‚ąěūĚĎĎő¶‚Äč(t)==1‚ąíC‚Äč(4‚ąí6‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC+őľ2‚Äčc2‚ÄčC2‚ąí2‚ÄčC+2‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC2)2‚ąí4‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC+őľ2‚Äčc2‚ÄčC2.ūĚĎĚūĚĖĮūĚĎčūĚĎź1superscriptsubscript0differential-dő¶ūĚĎ°1ūĚź∂46ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚź∂22ūĚź∂2ūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚź∂224ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚź∂2p=\mathsf{P}\{X=c\}=1-\int_{0}^{\infty}d\Phi(t)=\\ =1-\frac{C(4-6\mu cC+\mu^{2}c^{2}C^{2}-2C+2\mu cC^{2})}{2-4\mu cC+\mu^{2}c^{2}C^{2}}.

Now we have

őĽ=c‚Äčp+‚ąę0‚ąět‚ÄčūĚĎĎő¶‚Äč(t)=c+2‚ÄčC3őľ‚Äč(2‚ąí4‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚ÄčC+őľ2‚Äčc2‚ÄčC2).ūĚúÜūĚĎźūĚĎĚsuperscriptsubscript0ūĚĎ°differential-dő¶ūĚĎ°ūĚĎź2superscriptūĚź∂3ūĚúá24ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚź∂superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚź∂2\lambda=cp+\int_{0}^{\infty}td\Phi(t)=c+\frac{2C^{3}}{\mu(2-4\mu cC+\mu^{2}c^{2}C^{2})}.

Finally, substitution C=e‚ąíőľ‚ÄčcūĚź∂superscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎźC=e^{-\mu c} gives the result

őĽ=c+2‚Äče‚ąí3‚Äčőľ‚Äčcőľ‚Äč(2‚ąí4‚Äčőľ‚Äčc‚Äče‚ąíőľ‚Äčc+őľ2‚Äčc2‚Äče‚ąí2‚Äčőľ‚Äčc).ūĚúÜūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎí3ūĚúáūĚĎźūĚúá24ūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚúáūĚĎźsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎí2ūĚúáūĚĎź\lambda=c+\frac{2e^{-3\mu c}}{\mu(2-4\mu ce^{-\mu c}+\mu^{2}c^{2}e^{-2\mu c})}.

A.2 A Matrix With Zero Row

Consider a solution for system (4) with the matrix

A‚Äč(k)=(őĪkc00),ūĚźīūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎź00A(k)=\left(\begin{array}[]{cc}\alpha_{k}&c\\ 0&0\end{array}\right),

where cūĚĎźc is a nonnegative constant, and őĪksubscriptūĚõľūĚĎė\alpha_{k} is a nonnegative r.v. that has a distribution function F‚Äč(t)ūĚźĻūĚĎ°F(t) with finite mean aūĚĎéa. First, we represent equations (1) in the form

x‚Äč(k)ūĚĎ•ūĚĎė\displaystyle x(k) =max‚Ā°(x‚Äč(k‚ąí1)+őĪk,y‚Äč(k‚ąí1)+c),absentūĚĎ•ūĚĎė1subscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎ¶ūĚĎė1ūĚĎź\displaystyle=\max(x(k-1)+\alpha_{k},y(k-1)+c),
y‚Äč(k)ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎė\displaystyle y(k) =max‚Ā°(x‚Äč(k‚ąí1),y‚Äč(k‚ąí1)).absentūĚĎ•ūĚĎė1ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎė1\displaystyle=\max(x(k-1),y(k-1)).

By introducing a new state variable

X‚Äč(k)=x‚Äč(k)‚ąíx‚Äč(k‚ąí1),ūĚĎčūĚĎėūĚĎ•ūĚĎėūĚĎ•ūĚĎė1X(k)=x(k)-x(k-1),

we reduce (1) to one equation

X‚Äč(k)=max‚Ā°(őĪk,c‚ąíX‚Äč(k‚ąí1)).ūĚĎčūĚĎėsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎėūĚĎźūĚĎčūĚĎė1X(k)=\max(\alpha_{k},c-X(k-1)).

Since x‚Äč(k)‚Č•y‚Äč(k)ūĚĎ•ūĚĎėūĚĎ¶ūĚĎėx(k)\geq y(k) and x‚Äč(k)=X‚Äč(1)+‚čĮ+X‚Äč(k)ūĚĎ•ūĚĎėūĚĎč1‚čĮūĚĎčūĚĎėx(k)=X(1)+\cdots+X(k), we have

őĽ=limk‚Üí‚ąě1k‚ÄčūĚĖ§‚Äčmax‚Ā°(x‚Äč(k),y‚Äč(k))=limk‚Üí‚ąě1k‚Äč‚ąĎi=1kūĚĖ§‚ÄčX‚Äč(k).ūĚúÜsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎė1ūĚĎėūĚĖ§ūĚĎ•ūĚĎėūĚĎ¶ūĚĎėsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎė1ūĚĎėsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĖ1ūĚĎėūĚĖ§ūĚĎčūĚĎė\lambda=\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{1}{k}\mathsf{E}\max(x(k),y(k))=\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\mathsf{E}X(k).

Furthermore, we define the distribution function

ő¶k‚Äč(t)=ūĚĖĮ‚Äč{X‚Äč(k)<t},subscriptő¶ūĚĎėūĚĎ°ūĚĖĮūĚĎčūĚĎėūĚĎ°\Phi_{k}(t)=\mathsf{P}\{X(k)<t\},

and note that

ő¶0‚Äč(t)={0,if¬†t‚Č§0,1,if¬†t>0,ő¶1‚Äč(t)={0,if¬†t‚Č§c,F‚Äč(t),if¬†t>c.formulae-sequencesubscriptő¶0ūĚĎ°cases0if¬†t‚Č§01if¬†t>0subscriptő¶1ūĚĎ°cases0if¬†t‚Č§cūĚźĻūĚĎ°if¬†t>c\Phi_{0}(t)=\begin{cases}0,&\text{if $t\leq 0$},\\ 1,&\text{if $t>0$},\end{cases}\qquad\Phi_{1}(t)=\begin{cases}0,&\text{if $t\leq c$},\\ F(t),&\text{if $t>c$}.\end{cases}

Evaluation of the above probability for all k‚Č•1ūĚĎė1k\geq 1 gives a recursive equation

ő¶k‚Äč(t)={0,if¬†t‚Č§0,F‚Äč(t)‚Äč(1‚ąíő¶k‚ąí1‚Äč(c‚ąít)),if¬†0<t‚Č§c,F‚Äč(t),if¬†t>c.subscriptő¶ūĚĎėūĚĎ°cases0if¬†t‚Č§0ūĚźĻūĚĎ°1subscriptő¶ūĚĎė1ūĚĎźūĚĎ°if¬†0<t‚Č§cūĚźĻūĚĎ°if¬†t>c\Phi_{k}(t)=\begin{cases}0,&\text{if $t\leq 0$},\\ F(t)(1-\Phi_{k-1}(c-t)),&\text{if $0<t\leq c$},\\ F(t),&\text{if $t>c$}.\end{cases}

Let us examine the equation when 0<t‚Č§c0ūĚĎ°ūĚĎź0<t\leq c. After one iteration, we have

ő¶k‚Äč(t)=F‚Äč(t)‚Äč(1‚ąíF‚Äč(c‚ąít)‚Äč(1‚ąíő¶k‚ąí2‚Äč(t))).subscriptő¶ūĚĎėūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°1subscriptő¶ūĚĎė2ūĚĎ°\Phi_{k}(t)=F(t)(1-F(c-t)(1-\Phi_{k-2}(t))).

Now we define a function

G‚Äč(t)=F‚Äč(t)‚ÄčF‚Äč(c‚ąít).ūĚźļūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°G(t)=F(t)F(c-t).

For all even k=2‚ÄčmūĚĎė2ūĚĎök=2m, by iterating the last equation, we arrive at the function

ő¶2‚Äčm‚Äč(t)=1‚ąí(1‚ąíF‚Äč(t))‚Äč(1+G‚Äč(t)+‚čĮ+Gm‚ąí1‚Äč(t)).subscriptő¶2ūĚĎöūĚĎ°11ūĚźĻūĚĎ°1ūĚźļūĚĎ°‚čĮsuperscriptūĚźļūĚĎö1ūĚĎ°\Phi_{2m}(t)=1-(1-F(t))(1+G(t)+\cdots+G^{m-1}(t)).

The sum on the right-hand side is actually an initial part of the geometric series with the common ratio G‚Äč(t)<1ūĚźļūĚĎ°1G(t)<1.

In the limit as mūĚĎöm tends to infinity, for all tūĚĎ°t under consideration, the subsequence ő¶2‚Äčm‚Äč(t)subscriptő¶2ūĚĎöūĚĎ°\Phi_{2m}(t) goes to

ő¶‚Äč(t)=1‚ąí1‚ąíF‚Äč(t)1‚ąíG‚Äč(t)=F‚Äč(t)‚ąíG‚Äč(t)1‚ąíG‚Äč(t).ő¶ūĚĎ°11ūĚźĻūĚĎ°1ūĚźļūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚźļūĚĎ°1ūĚźļūĚĎ°\Phi(t)=1-\frac{1-F(t)}{1-G(t)}=\frac{F(t)-G(t)}{1-G(t)}.

In the same way, for all k=2‚Äčm+1ūĚĎė2ūĚĎö1k=2m+1, we get a subsequence of functions

ő¶2‚Äčm+1‚Äč(t)=F‚Äč(t)‚Äč(1‚ąíF‚Äč(c‚ąít))‚Äč(1+G‚Äč(t)+‚čĮ+Gm‚ąí1‚Äč(t)),subscriptő¶2ūĚĎö1ūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°1ūĚźļūĚĎ°‚čĮsuperscriptūĚźļūĚĎö1ūĚĎ°\Phi_{2m+1}(t)=F(t)(1-F(c-t))(1+G(t)+\cdots+G^{m-1}(t)),

and then verify that ő¶2‚Äčm+1‚Äč(t)‚Üíő¶‚Äč(t)‚Üísubscriptő¶2ūĚĎö1ūĚĎ°ő¶ūĚĎ°\Phi_{2m+1}(t)\to\Phi(t) as m‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜíūĚĎöm\to\infty.

From the convergence of the subsequences to a common limit, it follows that when 0<t‚Č§c0ūĚĎ°ūĚĎź0<t\leq c, the entire sequence ő¶k‚Äč(t)subscriptő¶ūĚĎėūĚĎ°\Phi_{k}(t) also converges to

ő¶‚Äč(t)=F‚Äč(t)‚ąíG‚Äč(t)1‚ąíG‚Äč(t)=F‚Äč(t)‚Äč(1‚ąíF‚Äč(c‚ąít))1‚ąíF‚Äč(t)‚ÄčF‚Äč(c‚ąít).ő¶ūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚźļūĚĎ°1ūĚźļūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°\Phi(t)=\frac{F(t)-G(t)}{1-G(t)}=\frac{F(t)(1-F(c-t))}{1-F(t)F(c-t)}.

Taking into account that ő¶k‚Äč(t)=F‚Äč(t)subscriptő¶ūĚĎėūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎ°\Phi_{k}(t)=F(t) if t>cūĚĎ°ūĚĎźt>c, we finally conclude that for all tūĚĎ°t, as k‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜíūĚĎėk\to\infty, the sequence {ő¶k}subscriptő¶ūĚĎė\{\Phi_{k}\} converges to the function

ő¶‚Äč(t)={0,if¬†t‚Č§0,F‚Äč(t)‚Äč(1‚ąíF‚Äč(c‚ąít))1‚ąíF‚Äč(t)‚ÄčF‚Äč(c‚ąít),if¬†0<t‚Č§c,F‚Äč(t),if¬†t>c.ő¶ūĚĎ°cases0if¬†t‚Č§0ūĚźĻūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°if¬†0<t‚Č§cūĚźĻūĚĎ°if¬†t>c\Phi(t)=\begin{cases}0,&\text{if $t\leq 0$},\\ \frac{F(t)(1-F(c-t))}{1-F(t)F(c-t)},&\text{if $0<t\leq c$},\\ F(t),&\text{if $t>c$}.\end{cases}

It is clear that the limiting function ő¶‚Äč(t)ő¶ūĚĎ°\Phi(t) presents a distribution function for a r.v. XūĚĎčX.

Since X‚Äč(k)ūĚĎčūĚĎėX(k) converges to XūĚĎčX in distribution, we see that ūĚĖ§‚ÄčX‚Äč(k)‚ÜíūĚĖ§‚ÄčX‚ÜíūĚĖ§ūĚĎčūĚĎėūĚĖ§ūĚĎč\mathsf{E}X(k)\to\mathsf{E}X as k‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜíūĚĎėk\to\infty, and therefore, őĽ=ūĚĖ§‚ÄčXūĚúÜūĚĖ§ūĚĎč\lambda=\mathsf{E}X.

To evaluate the expected value of XūĚĎčX, consider the integral

‚ąę0‚ąět‚ÄčūĚĎĎő¶‚Äč(t)=‚ąę0ct‚Äčd‚Äč(F‚Äč(t)‚Äč(1‚ąíF‚Äč(c‚ąít))1‚ąíF‚Äč(t)‚ÄčF‚Äč(c‚ąít)‚ąíF‚Äč(t))+‚ąę0‚ąět‚ÄčūĚĎĎF‚Äč(t).superscriptsubscript0ūĚĎ°differential-dő¶ūĚĎ°superscriptsubscript0ūĚĎźūĚĎ°ūĚĎĎūĚźĻūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎ°superscriptsubscript0ūĚĎ°differential-dūĚźĻūĚĎ°\int_{0}^{\infty}td\Phi(t)=\int_{0}^{c}td\left(\frac{F(t)(1-F(c-t))}{1-F(t)F(c-t)}-F(t)\right)+\int_{0}^{\infty}tdF(t).

By applying integration by parts to the first term, we finally get

őĽ=a+‚ąę0cF‚Äč(t)‚ÄčF‚Äč(c‚ąít)‚Äč(1‚ąíF‚Äč(t))1‚ąíF‚Äč(t)‚ÄčF‚Äč(c‚ąít)‚ÄčūĚĎĎt.ūĚúÜūĚĎésuperscriptsubscript0ūĚĎźūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎ°1ūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚźĻūĚĎźūĚĎ°differential-dūĚĎ°\lambda=a+\int_{0}^{c}\frac{F(t)F(c-t)(1-F(t))}{1-F(t)F(c-t)}dt.
